info.name: SaaS Platform API v1
info.description:
Multi-tenant SaaS platform (Laravel 13). Generated from the OpenAPI spec.

**Two headers do the work.** `Authorization: Bearer {{token}}` (set automatically by Register/Login) says who you are; `X-Organization: {{org_slug}}` says which organization to act in — required on every tenant-scoped request.

Public endpoints send no token. Query filters are included but disabled; enable the ones you need. Path variables (`:id`, `:customer`, …) default to placeholders — override them per request.

`org_slug` defaults to `acme-inc` (a seeded demo organization from `php artisan app:demo`) so tenant-scoped requests work immediately. Running **Sign in** as a member overwrites it with that user's first organization. If you see *"Organization context required"*, `org_slug` is empty — set it or sign in first.

item.name: Verify an email address from a signed link

request.description:
Verify an email address from a signed link

Signed rather than authenticated: the link is clicked from a mail client, where no bearer token exists. The signature plus a hash of the current address proves ownership.

Re-send the verification email

Re-send the verification email to the signed-in user.

Request a password-reset link

Always returns the same message whether or not the address is registered, so this cannot be used to enumerate users. Rate limited to 6/minute.

Sign in

Returns a token, or 202 with a challenge when the account has two-factor enabled. Five failed attempts per email+IP trigger a 15-minute lockout (429).

Sign out of this device

Revokes only the token that made this request, so other devices stay signed in. Use the sessions endpoints to revoke elsewhere.

item.name: The authenticated user and the organizations they can switch between

request.description:
The authenticated user and the organizations they can switch between

The authenticated user plus the organizations they can switch between.

Change your password

Requires the current password. Every other session is revoked on success, so a password change actually evicts an attacker who already holds a token; the calling device stays signed in.

item.name: Register a user and create their first organization

body.raw:
{
    "name": "Ada Lovelace",
    "email": "user@example.test",
    "password": "Min 12 chars with mixed case, a number, and a symbol.",
    "password_confirmation": "string",
    "organization_name": "Acme Inc",
    "timezone": "UTC"
}

request.description:
Register a user and create their first organization

Creates the user, provisions a dedicated database for the organization, seeds its roles, makes the caller its owner, and returns an API token. Rate limited to 6/minute.

item.name: Reset a password using an emailed token

request.description:
Reset a password using an emailed token

All existing API tokens are revoked: a reset implies the account may have been compromised.

Active sessions (issued tokens)

Lets a user spot unfamiliar devices and revoke them. The current session is flagged.

Revoke one session

Scoped to your own tokens, so one user cannot sign another out.

item.name: Revoke every session except the current one

request.description:
Revoke every session except the current one

Revoke every session except the current one.

Recent authentication attempts

Successes and failures for the signed-in user. Paginated.

Complete a two-factor sign-in

Exchanges the challenge_token from a 202 login, plus either a TOTP code or a recovery code, for an API token. Send one of code or recovery_code. A wrong code does not burn the challenge, but five wrong codes destroy it and sign-in restarts from the password. A TOTP code works only once: its time step is recorded and re-presenting it is refused, so observing a code is not enough to reuse it inside its 30-second window.

Two-factor status

Reports whether enrolment is unstarted, pending confirmation, or active, and how many recovery codes remain. The secret itself is never returned here — only the enable step ever discloses it.

Disable two-factor authentication

Requires the account password: a stolen bearer token must not be enough to remove the factor that exists to survive a stolen password. Clears the secret and every recovery code.

Begin two-factor enrolment

Returns a secret and an otpauth:// URI to render as a QR code. This does NOT protect the account yet — call confirm with a code from the authenticator to finish. Calling this again discards any half-finished enrolment and issues a fresh secret, so an abandoned QR code cannot be confirmed later.

item.name: Finish enrolment and get recovery codes

request.description:
Finish enrolment and get recovery codes

Proves the authenticator works, and only then does 2FA start guarding sign-in. Recovery codes are returned here and are shown in full exactly once at this point; they are the way back in when the device is lost.

View remaining recovery codes

Codes are stored encrypted rather than hashed precisely so they can be shown again here — a user who lost the list but still has their authenticator would otherwise have no way to recover one.

Replace the recovery codes

Issues a fresh set and invalidates every previous code — the correct move once a list has been printed, shared, or partially spent. Requires the password.

Stop impersonating

Revokes the current impersonation token immediately — before its own expiry would — and records the end of the session. Returns 409 if the current token is an ordinary login rather than an impersonation session.

Super Admin audit trail

Every suspend, edit, delete, restore, and purge, newest first. Central and cross-org — distinct from the per-organization activity log that lives inside each tenant database. Filterable by action and by target organization.

item.name: Impersonate a member of an organization

request.description:
Impersonate a member of an organization

Returns a short-lived token that acts as the target user (an owner by default, or the named member) inside THIS organization only. The token cannot reach the admin API and cannot enter the target user's other organizations. A super admin can never be impersonated. Both start and stop are written to the admin audit trail.

query.description: Organization name, phone, or owner name/email

request.description:
List every organization

Super-admin only. Central-context: reads across all organizations without entering any tenant database. Searchable by organization name, phone, or owner name/email; filterable by status, plan, and registration date; sortable on an allowlist. Cross-tenant metrics (projects, storage) are null until the Phase 2 stats rollup lands.

Platform-wide dashboard counters

Super-admin only. Organization counts by lifecycle state plus distinct users across the platform. Cross-tenant totals (projects, storage) are null pending the Phase 2 rollup — null means not-yet-measured, distinct from a real zero.
One organization in full

Edit an organization profile

The slug is intentionally not editable — it is the identifier clients send in X-Organization, and changing it would break every integration. Status changes go through the suspend/activate endpoints, not this one.

Soft-delete an organization

Reversible. The central row is trashed — which cuts member access immediately — but the physical tenant database is KEPT, so the org can be restored. The database is only ever dropped later, by the tenants:purge command, after a retention window. A "soft delete" here never destroys data.

Override an organization's plan limits

Give one organization different ceilings than its plan without inventing a bespoke plan. The body replaces the whole override map: a key present overrides the plan (an integer, or null for unlimited); a key absent falls back to the plan. Send an empty object to clear all overrides. All quota enforcement — uploads, record creation — honours these immediately.

Suspend an organization

Cuts access immediately: the tenant middleware refuses a suspended organization on its next request, so no token needs to expire. Reversible via activate.

Reactivate a suspended organization

Restores to active, never to trial — reviving a spent trial would grant a second free run.

Restore a soft-deleted organization

Brings a trashed organization and its members back online. Because the database was never dropped, nothing is re-provisioned — the data is exactly as it was left.

Organizations the caller belongs to

Drives the organization switcher. Needs no X-Organization header.

Create an additional organization

Provisions a dedicated database, seeds its roles, and makes the caller its owner.

Update the active organization settings

POST rather than PUT because the logo is a multipart upload, which PHP does not parse for PUT. The slug is immutable: it is the tenant identifier clients send in X-Organization.

item.name: Your role and permissions in the active organization

request.description:
Your role and permissions in the active organization

The SPA uses the permission list to show or hide controls. The API re-authorizes every request regardless, so this is never the security boundary.

Preview an invitation

Public: the recipient opens the emailed link before signing in, so the SPA must show which organization invited them and to which address. Returns no sensitive fields.

Accept an invitation

Outside tenant scope on purpose: the invitee is not a member yet, so the tenant middleware would reject them before they could join. Acceptance is bound to the invited address.

Members of the active organization

Each member is shown with the role they hold HERE. The same person may hold a different role in another organization.

Pending invitations

Pending invitations for this organization.

Invite someone by email

Sends an emailed link with a single-use token valid for 7 days. Re-inviting the same address replaces the previous invitation. Owner can only be granted by an owner.
Revoke a pending invitation

item.name: Change a member role in this organization

request.description:
Change a member role in this organization

An organization must always keep at least one owner; demoting the last one is refused.

item.name: Remove a member from this organization

request.description:
Remove a member from this organization

Their role here is revoked; their other organizations are untouched. You cannot remove yourself, nor the last owner.

item.name: Aggregate metrics for the active organization

request.description:
Aggregate metrics for the active organization

Customer totals and status breakdown, active-customer lifetime value, a zero-filled six-month growth series, and the five most recent customers. Cached for 5 minutes per organization.

item.name: List customers in the active organization

query.description: Search name, email, company, or phone

request.description:
List customers in the active organization

Paginated list supporting search, filtering, and sorting.
Create a customer

item.name: One customer, with tags, notes, and attachments

request.description: One customer, with tags, notes, and attachments

Update a customer

Partial update. Omitting `tags` leaves them unchanged; sending an empty array removes them all.

Delete a customer (soft)

Soft delete: recoverable via the restore endpoint.
Restore a soft-deleted customer

item.name: Export the current selection as CSV

request.description:
Export the current selection as CSV

Streamed and chunked, so a large organization does not exhaust memory. Accepts the same filters as the list endpoint.

Import customers from a CSV

Rows are validated individually: a bad row is reported and skipped rather than aborting the whole file. A `name` column is required.

item.name: Add a note to a customer

request.description:
Add a note to a customer

Attach a note to a customer.

List projects

Includes task counts and a progress percentage, loaded with withCount so a list does not fire a query per project.

Create a project

Ownership defaults to the creator. Setting status to completed stamps completed_at automatically.

item.name: One project with members and attachments

request.description: One project with members and attachments

Update a project

status and completed_at are kept in step: marking complete stamps the date, reopening clears it.

Delete a project (soft)

Tasks are left intact so the project can be restored; cascading would orphan their time entries.
Restore a soft-deleted project

query.description: Allowlisted column; prefix with - to reverse

request.description:
List tasks

The flat, paginated view behind the table and calendar. Use /tasks/board for the Kanban shape. Sorting is restricted to an allowlist of columns rather than passed through to the query.

Create a task

A task with a parent_id is a subtask. Creation counts against the plan task quota, which is why the failure here is 402 rather than 403: the caller is permitted, the plan is not.

Tasks grouped into Kanban columns

Every column is returned even when empty, so the board renders its full shape. One query for the whole board, grouped in PHP.

item.name: One task with subtasks, comments, and time entries

request.description: One task with subtasks, comments, and time entries

Update a task

Setting status to done stamps completed_at; moving away from done clears it.

Delete a task (soft)

Subtasks are soft-deleted with the parent, so they do not resurface at the root of the board.

item.name: Move a card on the Kanban board

request.description:
Move a card on the Kanban board

Positions are floats: a card dropped between two others takes their midpoint, so a drag writes one row rather than renumbering the column. Omit before_id to append to the end.

item.name: Start a timer on a task

request.description:
Start a timer on a task

A user has at most one running timer; starting a new one stops any other. MySQL has no partial unique index, so this is enforced in the application.

item.name: Stop your running timer on a task

request.description: Stop your running timer on a task

Log time after the fact

Records a completed entry without running a timer. The start is back-dated so the entry occupies real time on a timeline.

item.name: Your currently running timer, if any

request.description:
Your currently running timer, if any

Drives the persistent timer widget. Returns null when nothing is running.

Time entries for a task

Entries for a task.

Remove a time entry

You may only remove your own time unless you can delete tasks: a timesheet is a billing record, so one person must not quietly rewrite anothers.

item.name: Calendar occurrences in a date window

query.description: Required. Window end; max one year after from

request.description:
Calendar occurrences in a date window

Returns EXPANDED occurrences, not rows: a recurring series is one row but many occurrences. The window is capped at one year so a malformed rule cannot expand unbounded.

item.name: Create an event or recurring series

request.description:
Create an event or recurring series

Recurrence is stored as a rule and expanded on read. Omit recurrence_frequency for a one-off.

item.name: One event row with attendees and reminders

request.description: One event row with attendees and reminders

Update an event or series

Updates the whole series. To change one occurrence only, use the occurrence endpoint.
Delete an event or series

item.name: Cancel or move ONE occurrence of a series

request.description:
Cancel or move ONE occurrence of a series

Creates an exception row keyed to the occurrence original start; the expander then skips the generated one. The rest of the series is untouched.

item.name: Attach a file to a customer

request.description:
Attach a file to a customer

Executable and script types are refused regardless of the MIME type claimed, because the browser-reported type is attacker-controlled and an .html or .svg served from our origin is stored XSS. The stored path is generated; the original filename is kept only as a label.

Delete an attachment

Scoped to the customer, so an id from another record cannot be deleted. Removes the stored bytes as well as the row.

Browse a folder

Contents of a folder (root when folder_id is omitted), a breadcrumb built from the materialised path, and a storage summary for the quota bar.

Upload a file

Enforces the plan storage quota (402 when it would be exceeded) and refuses executable or script types regardless of the MIME type claimed. The stored path is generated, never the client filename.
Download a file

Delete a file (soft)

Soft delete: the bytes stay until the record is force-deleted, so a restore does not hand back a row pointing at nothing.
Create a folder

item.name: Delete a folder and its subtree

request.description: Delete a folder and its subtree

Create a public share link

The token is returned only in the URL and stored hashed. The organization slug is part of the link because a public visitor sends no header that could say which tenant database holds the share.

Share metadata

Public. Lets the download page render (and prompt for a password when required) before fetching the bytes. Tenancy is resolved from the organization slug in the path.

Download a shared file

Public. POST rather than GET so a password can be supplied in the body. Increments the download counter.

item.name: Your in-app notifications for the active organization

request.description:
Your in-app notifications for the active organization

Notifications live in the tenant database, so the same user has a separate inbox per organization: activity in one org never appears in another.
Mark one notification as read
Mark every notification as read
Remove a notification

List webhook endpoints

Secrets are never returned here: like an API key, the secret is shown once at creation only.

Create a webhook endpoint

Returns the signing secret ONCE. Deliveries are signed with HMAC-SHA256 over the exact body in an X-Signature header, so a receiver can prove the payload is ours.

Update a webhook endpoint

Re-enabling an endpoint that was auto-paused resets its failure count, so it does not pause again on the next failure regardless of the fix.
Remove a webhook endpoint

Recent delivery attempts

The debugging view: status code, error, and attempt number for the last 50 deliveries.

Available plans

Amounts are for display only; Stripe is the source of truth for what is actually charged. Stripe price ids are never exposed. A null limit means unlimited, which is different from 0.

Subscription, usage, and payment method

Renders entirely from local state: an organization that has never paid has no Stripe customer, and the billing page must not depend on Stripe uptime.

A Stripe SetupIntent client secret

Stripe.js uses this to collect card details in the browser. Raw card data never touches our servers, which is what keeps this application out of PCI scope.

Start a subscription

payment_method is a Stripe payment-method id from Stripe.js, never a card number. Remaining trial is preserved rather than restarted, and an organization that already subscribed once does not get a second free trial.

Change plan or billing interval

Proration is invoiced immediately rather than accruing silently onto the next invoice: an upgrade the customer just chose should not surprise them a month later.

item.name: Cancel at the end of the period

request.description:
Cancel at the end of the period

Access continues until the period ends (the grace period): cancelling must not destroy access already paid for. Use resume to undo.

Resume a cancelled subscription

Only possible during the grace period; afterwards a new subscription is required.

Invoice history

Read live from Stripe rather than kept as our own copy of records Stripe owns. Empty for an organization with no Stripe customer.

Download an invoice PDF

Stream a single invoice PDF.

The organization audit trail

Read-only by design: an audit log you can edit is not an audit log. Entries live in the tenant database, so the trail is naturally scoped to one organization. Updates record only the fields that actually changed.
